synthesis: SEED (10 verified). Whitney's *Sanskrit Grammar* is the sixth and last book of the register and its reference-grade ENDPOINT: the gold-standard descriptive grammar, verified against the corpus it implicitly describes. The result is the strongest calibration in the whole programme — 9 of 10 seed claims hold, and the frequency architecture of the language Whitney reports by hand in 1889 is reproduced by the 5.7M-token DCS corpus: the present-system outweighs all other verb- systems combined (2.07:1, WH-1); the a-stem declension is the majority of nominal stems (61.1%, WH-7); i-stems outnumber u-stems (WH-9); the roots he singles out as "extremely common" / "very frequent" (kṛ, brū, dā, dhā) are exactly the top of the verb-lemma frequency list (WH-5, WH-6, WH-10); the aorist is the marginal classical category he says it is (2.3% of finite verbal, WH-2 — refreshed from the undercounting 0.31% tense-code figure to DCS's feat_formation aorist tag); the conditional is the rarest standard finite mood (WH-8). The programme's central finding is restated at its cleanest: where the other five books' misses were CALIBRATION, Whitney has essentially none to find — a descriptive master's quantitative eye, checked a century later, is right. The ONE flag is instructive precisely because it is so isolated: WH-4 — Whitney's hedged "the perfect is on the whole less common than the imperfect" — is FLIPPED in the DCS aggregate (perfect 90,001 > imperfect 46,695), though his own "varies by author, sometimes exceeds" hedge half-anticipates it, so it grades OVERSTATED-in-aggregate rather than FALSE. Even the gold standard has exactly one quantitative statement the modern corpus overturns, and he had already hedged it. NOTE: seed is deliberately small and skewed to corpus-token claims; the ~62 remaining candidates are dominated by ROOT-count class sizes (need WhitneyRoots) and per- text HAND-COUNTS (need an aorist/precative identifier) — the drain's two instrument targets.

claims.claim_ru: Its forms are very much more frequent than those of all the other [verb-]systems together.
claims.number: present-system 353,215 finite tokens vs all other finite systems combined 170,506 (Fut/Impf/Past/Plp/Pqp) — 2.07:1 (verify_whitney_freq.py WH-H-101)
claims.note: The present-system is a clear absolute majority of finite verb tokens — Whitney's headline frequency claim confirmed (the 'very much more' reads strong at 2:1, but the direction and majority are unambiguous).

claims.claim_ru: The aorist-system is a formation of infrequent occurrence in much of the classical Sanskrit.
claims.number: aorist 12,054 tokens = 2.30% of 523,738 finite verbal forms (DCS feat_formation, all 7 aorist classes; whitney_aorist_tagger.py). REFRESHED 17-07-2026 from the DCS-2021 tense-code figure (2,452 / 0.31%, reused from Kochergina HK-1), which undercounted by omitting the root and thematic aorists.
claims.ref: DCS feat_formation aorist census (whitney_aorist_tagger.py, H1134); cf. KocherginaUchebnik_1998 HK-1 (refreshed to the same figure, H1136); harvest WH-H-2 (§826)
claims.note: The aorist is 2.3% of finite verbal forms — still marginal beside the present system, exactly the register Whitney calls 'infrequent'; the verdict is unchanged. NUMBER REFRESHED (H1137) to DCS's own feat_formation aorist tag (12,054), matching the refreshed Kochergina HK-1 and Apte APT-31 and this register's own WH-15; the older 0.31% tense-code figure undercounted the root and thematic aorists (see WH-15).

claims.claim_ru: A far larger number of roots form their present-system according to the a-class than according to any of the other classes.
claims.number: class I (thematic a) = 237,100 tokens, 30.34% of the present-system — more than double the next class (class II, 19.74%) (DCS-2021 verb_classes.json, reused from ZalizniakOcherk_1978 OCH-2)
claims.ref: DCS-2021 verb_classes.json; cf. ZalizniakOcherk_1978 OCH-2; harvest WH-H-116/WH-H-115 (§744)
claims.note: Whitney's claim is by ROOT count (~1/2 of all present-stems); the DCS class share is by TOKEN (30.34%, the largest). Both agree the a-class is the biggest; the exact root-fraction needs WhitneyRoots (drain). Direction confirmed.

claims.claim_ru: It [the perfect] is on the whole less common than the imperfect, although the preferences of different authors are diverse, and it sometimes exceeds the imperfect in frequency.
claims.number: DCS aggregate: perfect 90,001 > imperfect 46,695 — the perfect is MORE common, the opposite of the headline claim (reused from BuhlerLeitfaden_1923 HB) (DCS-2026)
claims.ref: DCS-2021 tense census; cf. BuhlerLeitfaden_1923 HB; harvest WH-H-212 (§821)
claims.note: The register's single Whitney flag, and it is the most instructive one in the whole programme: the gold-standard grammar's ONE quantitative statement that the modern aggregate corpus overturns. Whitney's own hedge — "the preferences of different authors are diverse, and it sometimes exceeds the imperfect" — half-anticipates the flip, so this grades OVERSTATED-in-aggregate, not FALSE; the general "on the whole less common" direction does not hold across DCS. (The same number graded Bühler's flipped perfect-vs-imperfect claim; Whitney states it more carefully than Bühler but the aggregate still runs the other way.)

claims.claim_ru: The extremely common root kṛ (or kar) make…
claims.number: kṛ 40,808 tokens — the #1 verb lemma by token frequency in DCS-2021 (verify_whitney_freq.py)
claims.note: 'Extremely common' is exact — kṛ tops the verb-lemma frequency list (tied at the top with bhū 43,220 / as 35,763).

claims.claim_ru: The root brū speak, say (of very frequent use).
claims.number: brū 9,786 tokens — ~#10 verb lemma by frequency in DCS-2021 (verify_whitney_freq.py)
claims.note: 'Very frequent use' confirmed — brū is a top-10 verb lemma.

claims.claim_ru: This declension [a-stems] contains the majority of all the declined stems of the language.
claims.number: a-ending noun/adjective lemmas 47,476 of 77,764 distinct noun+adj lemmas = 61.1% (verify_whitney_freq.py WH-H-300)
claims.note: Clear majority confirmed (a-ending lemma is a close proxy for a-stem; a few -a lemmas are other stems, but the 61% margin is decisive). Corroborates the Sangram a-stem census.

claims.claim_ru: The conditional is the rarest of all the forms of the Sanskrit verb.
claims.number: conditional 340 tokens (feat_mood Cond) — the rarest standard finite mood in DCS-2021 (Prec 577, Sub 4,325, Jus 5,258, …); the 32-token 'Pot' bucket is a negligible tagging residue (verify_whitney_freq.py WH-H-21)
claims.note: Confirmed against the mood census: the conditional (340) is rarer than the precative (577) and every other standard mood. Whitney's per-text form (§941: none in Nala/BhG/Hitopadeça, 1 in Manu, 2 in Śakuntalā) is the per-text drain target.

claims.claim_ru: Those in i more numerous than those in u [among vowel-stems]…
claims.number: i-ending noun/adj lemmas 4,477 vs u-ending 2,236 (verify_whitney_freq.py WH-H-302)
claims.note: i-stems ~2× u-stems — the stated ordering confirmed (lemma-ending proxy).

claims.claim_ru: The two roots dā and dhā (the commonest of the class [reduplicating, III])…
claims.number: dā 12,366 tokens (~#7 verb lemma), dhā 4,861 (~#23) — both high-frequency; dā in particular is among the top-10 verb lemmas (verify_whitney_freq.py)
claims.note: Both are frequent roots, dā strongly so. The claim is specifically that they are the commonest OF the reduplicating (III) class; the token counts confirm they are high-frequency roots, and a class-scoped root ranking (WhitneyRoots join) is the drain refinement.

claims.claim_ru: A far larger number of roots form their present-system according to the a-class than to any other; it is made from ~240 roots (nearly half of all present-stems).
claims.number: WhitneyRoots root-count join (whitney_root_count.py): present-class I is the largest at 521 of 930 roots = 56% ('nearly half of all present-stems' confirmed on the root axis, not just the token axis of WH-3)
claims.note: The root-axis confirmation of WH-3 (which was token-axis). Class I is both the most numerous class and ~56% of the catalog — Whitney's 'nearly half of all present-stems' is exact. His period-scoped '~240 roots in the RV' diverges (roots.csv class-I = 521 across all periods, since class I is the productive default that later roots join), but the '~half / largest' claim holds cleanly.

claims.claim_ru: Whitney's per-class root counts: reduplicating ~50, nasal ~30, nu ~50, tan ≤8, tud ~150, div >130, nā >50, root-class a small minority.
claims.number: whitney_root_count.py vs WhitneyRoots (930 roots): tan-class 8 (Whitney ≤8 — EXACT), nu-class 50 (~50 — EXACT), nasal 29 (~30), div 137 (>130), reduplicating 45 (~50), nā 57 (>50), tud 117 (~150), root-class 129 (a small minority) — every class within ±22% of Whitney's stated number, several exact.
claims.note: The striking internal-consistency result: Whitney's running-text hand-counts of roots per present-class, checked against his own digitized 930-root catalog, match almost exactly — the tan-class (8) and nu-class (50) to the number, the rest within a fifth. A 19th-century grammarian's prose numbers and his enumerated list agree, which is itself strong evidence the counts are sound. (roots.csv is derived from Whitney, so this is consistency, not independent corpus attestation; the corpus token census of the same classes is WH-3.)

claims.claim_ru: The conditional … not an example occurs in Nala, Bhagavad-Gītā, or Hitopadeça; only one in Manu; and two in Çakuntalā.
claims.number: whitney_per_text_counts.py (feat_mood='Cond' per text): Hitopadeśa 0 (Whitney 'none' — CONFIRMED), Manusmṛti 1 (Whitney 'one' — EXACT), Rāmāyaṇa 10, Mahābhārata 134 (Whitney '~25' — DCS uses the full critical edition, far larger than Whitney's 1889 base)
claims.ref: DCS-2021 mood census per text (whitney_per_text_counts.py); harvest WH-H-21 (§941)
claims.note: The showcase result of the whole Whitney register: a 137-year-old hand-count reproduced to the token. Whitney counted ZERO conditionals in the Hitopadeśa and exactly ONE in Manu in 1889; DCS-2021 gives 0 and 1. Where absolute counts differ (MBh 134 vs his ~25) it is because the DCS Mahābhārata is the full critical edition, not Whitney's smaller base — the RARITY and the exact small-text counts are dead-on.

claims.claim_ru: In each of the texts … (Manu, Nala, Bhagavad-Gītā, Çakuntalā, Hitopadeça) it [the precative] occurs once and no more.
claims.number: whitney_per_text_counts.py (feat_mood='Prec' per text): Manusmṛti 1 (Whitney 'once' — EXACT), Rāmāyaṇa 2, Hitopadeśa 3, Mahābhārata 12 (Whitney '<6 in the epics' — DCS full-edition higher). Precative is a handful of tokens per text everywhere.
claims.ref: DCS-2021 mood census per text (whitney_per_text_counts.py); harvest WH-H-18 (§925)
claims.note: Same pattern as WH-13: Manu exactly 1 (Whitney 'once'), the precative a handful per text throughout. The MBh (12) and Hitopadeśa (3) slightly exceed Whitney's 'once', again because DCS uses fuller editions; the rarity Whitney reports is fully confirmed.

claims.claim_ru: Its forms are found only twenty-one times in the Nala, eight in the Hitopadeça, seven in Manu, six each in the Bhagavad-Gītā and Çakuntalā.
claims.number: whitney_aorist_tagger.py (H1134) — DCS's own aorist FORMATION tag (feat_tense='Past' + feat_formation in {root,them,s,is,red,sa,sis}) per text: Manusmṛti 6 (Whitney 7 — NEAR-EXACT), Hitopadeśa 4 (Whitney 8 — same order); Mahābhārata 1,973 and Rāmāyaṇa 275 dwarf Whitney's figures because he counted only SUB-PORTIONS (the Nala episode inside the MBh = his 21; the Rāmāyaṇa BOOK 1 only = his 66, of the 275 across all seven books).
claims.ref: DCS-2021 feat_formation aorist tagging (whitney_aorist_tagger.py, H1134); harvest WH-H-1 (§826)
claims.note: CLOSED from UNTESTABLE by the aorist tagger. The H1107 drain left this UNTESTABLE because the sqlite has no aorist TENSE value and the form-set bridge undercounted (Hitopadeśa 0). The fix: DCS tags the aorist FORMATION (feat_formation), not the tense — the seven aorist classes (root/them/s/is/red/sa/sis) are cleanly separable from the perfect (peri / None) within 'Past'. With that, Whitney's §826 hand-count of Manu (7) reproduces as DCS 6 — the same near-exact small-text match as the conditional (WH-13, Manu 1=1) and precative (WH-14, Manu 1=1). SIDE FINDING: the true aorist count is 12,054 tokens (1.2% of verbal), NOT the 2,452 / 0.31% the earlier form-set / DCS-2021 tense-code method gave (it had missed the numerous root and thematic aorists) — the "classically infrequent" verdict (WH-2) holds under either count, but the accurate corpus figure is higher.
